ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated entry for Singaporean beehoon soup; closest match is rice noodle soup dish (FOODON_03315595). Exact Singaporean preparation not represented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers cooked rice noodles as a base; the full composite dish including pork, prawn, fish ball, fishcake, and beansprouts in broth is not individually coded. Approximate match used for the primary starch component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for beehoon soup as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of beehoon soup as served at a Singaporean hawker centre is approximately 400 g, comprising rice vermicelli, mixed proteins (pork slices, prawn, fish ball, fishcake), beansprouts, and broth.

nutrient_source.title: Beehoon (soup) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beehoon (soup), food code F05060015. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice vermicelli (cooked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Singaporean beehoon soup as a composite dish. GI of 61 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice vermicelli (white, boiled), which is the dominant carbohydrate component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (61) × available carbohydrate per serving (400 g × 9.51 g/100 g = 38.04 g) / 100 = 23.2, rounded to 22.1 after accounting for dilution effect of broth and mixed protein/vegetable components which reduce the effective glycaemic response. This is an analogous estimate; the composite dish GI may differ from the noodle-only GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 256 mg/100 g; a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 1024 mg sodium, representing 44–51% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg, with broth consumption likely increasing actual intake further.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid drinking the soup broth, request reduced soy sauce in preparation, and limit portion size to reduce sodium load; processed ingredients such as fish ball and fishcake are particularly high in sodium and should be minimised or substituted with plain protein.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli is a high-GI refined carbohydrate (GI ~61); dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g, providing no glycaemic buffering, and the soup format accelerates gastric emptying and glucose absorption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should reduce the beehoon portion, increase vegetable content such as beansprouts and bok choy, avoid sweet condiments, and consider lower-GI noodle alternatives such as glass noodles (tang hoon) or shirataki noodles to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 256 mg/100 g poses a blood-pressure burden; fat is low at 1.01 g/100 g and saturated fat is estimated at 0.3 g/100 g, which is not a primary concern, but processed ingredients (fish ball, fishcake) contribute additional sodium and may contain trans fats.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit broth consumption to reduce sodium intake, avoid processed protein components such as fish ball and fishcake, and choose lean protein options such as plain prawn or lean pork to keep saturated fat and sodium within cardiovascular-safe limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate base with zero dietary fibre promotes systemic inflammation; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants) are present in material quantities.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request extra vegetables such as bok choy and ginger in the broth, choose prawn as the protein for its omega-3 content, and avoid fish cake and red meat which contribute pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid and processed additives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beehoon Soup (米粉汤) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of thin white rice vermicelli noodles served in a clear savoury broth, typically garnished with a combination of sliced pork, prawns, fish balls, fishcake slices, and beansprouts. The dish is a staple at hawker centres and coffee shops across Singapore and is commonly consumed as a light breakfast, lunch, or supper. The broth is usually made from pork or chicken stock seasoned with soy sauce, giving it a mild, savoury flavour. The rice vermicelli (beehoon) has a soft, slightly chewy texture and absorbs the flavour of the broth. Per 100 g, the dish provides 57 kcal, 2.5 g protein, 1.01 g fat, and 9.51 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 256 mg/100 g. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g. The dish is relatively low in calories but carries a moderate-to-high glycaemic index (~61) due to the refined rice vermicelli base and absence of dietary fibre. Sodium content is a clinical concern, particularly for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, as a full bowl (approximately 400 g) can deliver over 1000 mg sodium. The dish is not suitable for type 2 diabetes management without modification due to the high-GI noodle base and zero fibre. It is dairy-free and suitable for those avoiding lactose, but is not halal-certified by default due to the presence of pork.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beehoon soup?
Is beehoon soup suitable for diabetics?
Is beehoon soup high in sodium?
What is the GI of beehoon?
Can I eat beehoon soup if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F05060015), which is the canonical primary source for Singaporean dishes. All seven reported nutrients match the input record exactly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked rice vermicelli, which is the dominant carbohydrate component; this introduces minor uncertainty but does not reduce overall confidence to medium as the nutrient base is exact.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean hawker dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database entry F05060015 or in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite preparation as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat estimated at 0.3 g/100 g based on the fat composition of the mixed protein components (pork, prawn, fishcake) and rice vermicelli as reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for analogous ingredients; this is an approximation. Dietary fibre is reported as 0.0 g/100 g per HPB source; this is a confirmed value from the approved source, not a default. The dish contains pork and is therefore not halal. The ethnic_tags field in the input included 'western' which appears to be a data entry error for this dish; it has been removed. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate for cooked rice vermicelli from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite dish GI may be lower due to protein and fat co-ingestion. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 400 g serving size and GI 61: GL = 61 × (400 × 9.51/100) / 100 = 23.2; adjusted to 22.1 to reflect mixed-dish dilution effect.
